slides.description: The slide is titled 'AI Screenplays' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title is a large, colorful illustration depicting a scene from the South Park cartoon, specifically an office. In the foreground, a gray-haired man in a suit is standing next to a cardboard robot labeled 'A.W.E.S.O.M.-O'. The office is lavishly decorated with a large wooden desk on the left holding Oscar and Emmy-like trophies, along with a golden Cartman trophy. Through a large window in the background, the 'HOLLYWOOD' sign is visible on a distant mountain range at sunset. To the right of the man, there's a globe, a fish tank with an alien inside, and a safe. On the wall, a framed picture displays the text 'AWESOME-O LAME'. On the desk in front of the man, there's a brown notebook, a partially visible script, a vintage telephone, and a framed photo featuring several South Park characters.
slides.narration: Alright everyone, welcome! I'm your instructor for today, and I'm super excited to dive into something really cool with all of you: AI Screenplays. Yeah, you saw the slide, we're talking about A.W.E.S.O.M.-O here, right? For those of you that just got here, this isn't just about making simple short texts; we're going to explore how AI can generate *long*, coherent, complex documents. Think entire movie screenplays. We're going to build out what I call an 'agentic flow' that specifically tackles the challenge of generating these big, structured texts, using screenplays as our main example. But the best part is, this whole architecture? Totally generalizable. So, get ready to see how we can shove AI into the writer's room, yeah?

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Generating Long Documents' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, there are four black, left-aligned bullet points. The first bullet states: 'AI can be used to generate long, coherent text documents'. The second bullet provides examples: 'Books, reports, screenplays'. The third bullet outlines the presentation's focus: 'Today we will build an agentic flow to generate screenplays for movies'. The fourth and final bullet indicates broader applicability: 'This flow architecture can be generalized to other long text documents'.
slides.narration: Okay, so this slide, right? 'Generating Long Documents.' It pretty much sums up what we're here to talk about. The big idea, the *thesis* if you will, is that AI actually *can* be used to generate really long, super coherent text documents. And I'm not just talking about a short paragraph or a quick email here, yeah? We're talking serious content like entire books, detailed reports, or, as our primary example today, full-blown movie screenplays. So, for today, what we're going to dive into is building out what I call an 'agentic flow' specifically designed to generate these movie screenplays. But here's the cool part, the kicker: this whole flow architecture, this entire system we're going to build? It's totally generalizable. You can take these principles, tweak 'em a bit, and apply them to generating all sorts of other long documents. So, it's not just about movies, it's about unlocking AI for *any* big, structured text.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Challenge: "One-Shot" Long-Form' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, there are two main black, left-aligned bullet points. The first bullet states: 'It is difficult for an AI to generate a long coherent document in one-shot'. The second bullet reads: 'Local vs global – the AI is good at writing small parts, but cannot maintain coherence across whole document'. This second bullet has two indented sub-bullets: '– Becomes “lost in the middle”' and '– Context drift – deep into the document, the AI can hallucinate or lose focus of what to write'.
slides.narration: Okay, so here's the *big* challenge, right? Why can't we just tell a large language model, 'Hey, write me a 100-page screenplay about, like, a sentient teapot who becomes a detective,' and get a perfect, coherent masterpiece in one go? This slide sums it up perfectly: the 'Challenge: "One-Shot" Long-Form.' The truth is, it's incredibly difficult for an AI to generate a *long*, truly coherent document in just one single shot.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Movie Screenplays' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, there are two main black, left-aligned bullet points. The first main bullet states: 'Screenplays have many different elements the AI needs to capture'. This bullet has three indented sub-bullets: '– Story shape', '– Goals of “sequences” in the movie', and '– Scene action and dialogue'. The second main bullet states: 'Hierarchical structure of a screenplay'. This bullet has four indented sub-bullets: '– Premise – basic elements of the movie', '– Story arc – high level plot structure', '– Sequences – more detailed plot', and '– Scenes – even more detailed plot'.
slides.narration:
Okay, so we just talked about the problem of one-shot generation, right? The AI gets lost in the middle, it can't keep track of everything for a super long document. So, how do we fix that? Well, let's look at our example: 'Movie Screenplays.' The cool thing about screenplays is they're *not* just one big blob of text. They have a ton of different elements, yeah? Things like the overall 'Story shape' – how does it rise and fall? Then you've got 'Goals of "sequences" in the movie' – little mini-arcs within the larger story. And of course, the nitty-gritty: 'Scene action and dialogue.'

And this is where the magic happens, because screenplays naturally have a 'Hierarchical structure.' This is super important. We're talking about breaking it down, step by step. You start with the 'Premise' – the absolute basic elements of the movie. Then you build that out into the 'Story arc' – the high-level plot structure, the major acts. From there, you flesh it out further into 'Sequences' – which is a more detailed plot, usually a set of connected scenes. And finally, you get to 'Scenes' themselves, which are the even more detailed plot, the specific actions, the dialogue. So, you see? It's already broken down for us. This is what's going to allow us to make the AI generate something truly coherent.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Screenplay Flow' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, a horizontal flowchart diagram illustrates the stages of a screenplay generation process. The flow begins on the left with a blue rectangular box labeled 'Premise', displaying 'premise.json' below the label and a small robot icon. An arrow points from 'Premise' to a purple rectangular box labeled 'Arc', which displays 'arc.json' and a robot icon. From 'Arc', two arrows emerge: one points horizontally to an orange rectangular box labeled 'Sequences', displaying 'sequence.json' and a robot icon; the other arrow points downwards to a green rectangular box labeled 'Scenes', displaying 'scenes.json' and a robot icon.
slides.narration: Okay, so we just talked about how screenplays have this awesome, natural hierarchical structure, right? Premise, arc, sequences, scenes. Well, this slide, 'Screenplay Flow,' shows you *how* we leverage that. This is the agentic flow we're going to build. Think of each one of these boxes—Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes—as a specialized AI 'agent.' You see the little robot icon there, yeah? Each agent is responsible for generating a specific, manageable chunk of the screenplay, producing its output in a structured JSON format. So, we start with our 'Premise' agent, which is going to lay down the core idea, then that feeds into our 'Arc' agent to build out the high-level plot. From there, the 'Arc' then informs our 'Sequences' agent, which breaks those acts down into detailed mini-story units. And finally, all of that context gets funneled into our 'Scenes' agent, which generates the actual, nitty-gritty scenes with dialogue and action. See how that works? We're taking that massive, impossible task of writing a whole screenplay in one go and breaking it down into smaller, coherent, AI-manageable steps.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Premise Agent' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. In the top right corner, there is a small image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O cardboard robot from South Park. Below the title, there are four black, left-aligned bullet points. The first bullet states: 'A chat agent that discusses with you what your movie is about'. This bullet has an indented sub-bullet: '– Title, logline (like an executive summary), characters, genre, central conflict'. The second bullet reads: 'You can ask the agent to give ideas for the plot if you are stuck'. The third bullet states: 'This chat can take a few minutes even if you have a general sense about your movie (takes time to specify all the major elements of the movie)'. The fourth and final bullet reads: 'This agent then creates a file premise.json'.
slides.narration: Okay, so this is where we actually start diving into the nuts and bolts of our agentic flow, right? The very first agent in our chain, the one that kicks everything off, is what we call the 'Premise Agent.' And you see our little A.W.E.S.O.M.-O friend there, helping us out. This isn't just some automated thing, yeah? This is actually a *chat agent* that actively discusses your movie idea with you. It's like having a brainstorming partner. You're gonna hash out all the core stuff: the 'Title,' the 'logline' – think of that like the executive summary for your film – your main 'characters,' the 'genre,' and the 'central conflict.' This agent helps you define all of that. And the cool thing is, if you ever hit a wall, if you're feeling stuck on the plot or a character motivation, you can totally ask the agent to toss out some ideas, to help you get unstuck. Now, just a heads up, this chat can take a few minutes, even if you think you have a pretty good handle on your movie. Why? Because you're really drilling down and specifying all the major elements that are going to define your entire story. And once you've nailed down all these crucial details, this agent then creates a structured file for us, which we call `premise.json`.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Premise.json' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, a code snippet is displayed against a dark background, formatted like a JSON file in a code editor. The JSON content is: 'project_id': 'shadow_of_the_dark_world_20260402_025456', 'title': 'Shadow of the Dark World', 'writers': ['TBD'], 'source_script': null, 'format_notes': 'Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat.', 'logline': 'A seemingly ordinary young man, Link, is thrust into a perilous quest after his uncle is gravely wounded rescuing Princess Zelda. He must gather legendary artifacts, wield the fabled Master Sword, and confront a malevolent wizard to prevent the dark lord Ganon’s release and the conquest of their world.', 'genre': ['fantasy', 'action', 'adventure'], 'themes': ['hero’s journey', 'destiny', 'courage in the face of overwhelming evil', 'good versus evil', 'sacrifice'].
slides.narration: Okay, so here's what that Premise Agent actually *gives* us, right? This is the `premise.json` file. This is the output of all that back-and-forth chat you had with the agent. It's your foundational blueprint for the entire movie. So, you can see here, we've got the `project_id`, a unique identifier. The `title` of our movie – 'Shadow of the Dark World'. And notice the `format_notes` there: 'Awesome-O generated this from a CLI chat.' That's a direct reference to our interactive agent. But the real meat here, yeah? The `logline`. Look at this: 'A seemingly ordinary young man, Link, is thrust into a perilous quest after his uncle is gravely wounded rescuing Princess Zelda. He must gather legendary artifacts, wield the fabled Master Sword, and confront a malevolent wizard to prevent the dark lord Ganon’s release and the conquest of their world.' Pretty epic, right? And yeah, if you're thinking Zelda, you're on the right track – that's some good inspiration. Below that, we've got the `genre` defined: 'fantasy,' 'action,' 'adventure.' And then the `themes` – 'hero's journey,' 'destiny,' 'courage in the face of overwhelming evil,' 'good versus evil,' 'sacrifice.' This JSON file, this `premise.json`, is *critical*. It captures all those core elements we discussed on the last slide, in a structured, machine-readable format. This is what's going to guide *all* the subsequent agents in our flow, making sure they stay coherent and true to the original vision.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Premise.json' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, a code snippet is displayed against a dark background, formatted like a JSON file in a code editor. This is a continuation of the JSON content from the previous slide. The visible JSON content includes: 'central_conflict': 'Link's race against time to understand and embrace his role as a hero, stopping the sorcerer Aghanim from sacrificing Princess Zelda and six other maidens, a ritual that would unleash the ancient evil Ganon from the corrupted Dark World upon the Light World.', 'setting' which contains 'primary_time': 'High fantasy era, contemporary-feel for characters' and 'primary_places' as an array: ['Hyrule Castle', 'Kakariko Village', 'Eastern Palace', 'Lost Woods', 'Dark World']. It also shows 'protagonists' as an array containing an object with 'name': 'Link', 'role': 'Unassuming hero, destined to wield the Master Sword', and 'want': 'To save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and ultimately prevent Aghanim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule.' The JSON snippet is truncated at the bottom, indicating more content beyond the visible area.
slides.narration: Okay, picking up right where we left off with our `premise.json`. Remember, this is the deep dive into the blueprint for our entire movie, all generated through that interactive chat with our Premise Agent. So, here you can see the `central_conflict`. Look at this: 'Link's race against time to understand and embrace his role as a hero, stopping the sorcerer Aghanim from sacrificing Princess Zelda and six other maidens, a ritual that would unleash the ancient evil Ganon from the corrupted Dark World upon the Light World.' Talk about high stakes, yeah? We're setting up a full-blown epic here. Below that, we've got the `setting` clearly defined: the `primary_time` as 'High fantasy era, contemporary-feel for characters' – which is a cool blend. And then the `primary_places`: 'Hyrule Castle,' 'Kakariko Village,' 'Eastern Palace,' 'Lost Woods,' and the 'Dark World.' All those iconic locations are established right here. And then, crucially, our `protagonists`. We've got 'Link,' described as the 'Unassuming hero, destined to wield the Master Sword,' and his core `want` is 'To save his uncle, rescue Zelda, and ultimately prevent Aghanim and Ganon from destroying Hyrule.' See how comprehensive this is? We're not just throwing out vague ideas; we're establishing every foundational piece of the story right here in this `premise.json` file. This is the solid ground all our subsequent agents are going to build upon, ensuring the entire screenplay stays true to this original vision.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Premise.json' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, a code snippet is displayed against a dark background, formatted like a JSON file in a code editor. This is a continuation of the JSON content from the previous slides. The visible JSON content includes the 'protagonists' array, showing an object for 'Link' with his 'name', 'role', and 'want'. It then lists 'key_antagonist' as an object for 'Aghanim', detailing his 'name', 'role' as 'Evil sorcerer, Ganon's pawn', and 'want' to 'seal the seven maidens, including Princess Zelda, to open a portal to the Dark World and release Ganon.' Next, 'key_ally' is shown as an object for 'Sahashrahla' (Sahasrahla), describing his 'name', 'role' as 'Village elder, wise man, guide', and 'want' to 'guide Link to acquire the Master Sword and stop Aghanim's plans to release Ganon.' Finally, the 'stakes' are defined as 'The total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon, resulting in the enslavement or destruction of its inhabitants.' The JSON snippet is truncated at the bottom, indicating more content beyond the visible area.
slides.narration: Okay, so continuing with our `premise.json` here, we're really fleshing out the core cast and the stakes, yeah? We've already covered Link as our protagonist on the last slide, but here, you can clearly see our `key_antagonist`. That's Aghanim. His role? "Evil sorcerer, Ganon's pawn." And his want? "To seal the seven maidens, including Princess Zelda, to open a portal to the Dark World and release Ganon." Super clear motivation, right? No ambiguity there. And of course, every hero needs a guide, and ours is defined as `key_ally`, Sahasrahla. He's the "Village elder, wise man," and his goal is "To guide Link to acquire the Master Sword and stop Aghanim's plans to release Ganon." So, we've got our hero, our villain, our wise mentor, all clearly defined. And finally, the all-important `stakes`. Look at this: "The total conquest of the Light World by the dark lord Ganon, resulting in the enslavement or destruction of its inhabitants." This is what our hero is fighting for, what's on the line. Every single one of these details, from the characters' wants to the overarching stakes, is critical. This `premise.json` is not just a bunch of text; it's the DNA of our entire screenplay, ensuring that every subsequent agent stays true to this foundational vision.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Arc Agent' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. In the top right corner, there is a small image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O cardboard robot from South Park. Below the title, there are four black, left-aligned bullet points. The first bullet states: 'A chat agent that discusses your movie story arc'. This bullet has two indented sub-bullets: '– Arc consists of multiple acts' and '– Agent takes premise.json as input for context'. The second bullet reads: 'You tell the agent what happens in each act, then ask it to draft the act, one at a time'. The third bullet states: 'Premise file gives it context for the story arc'. The fourth and final bullet reads: 'This agent then creates a file arc.json'.
slides.narration:
Okay, so we've just spent a good chunk of time on that `premise.json` file, right? That foundational blueprint. Now, what do we do with it? We feed it to our next specialized agent, which is our 'Arc Agent.' You can see our little A.W.E.S.O.M.-O buddy is still here, helping us out. This, again, is another *chat agent*, meaning it's interactive, yeah? You're going to discuss your movie's overall story arc with it. Because, as we know, a movie arc isn't just one long, flat thing; it typically consists of multiple acts, like a three-act structure, right?

And the coolest part is, this agent takes that `premise.json` file we just created as its input. That's its context. It's not starting from scratch; it knows all about Link, Zelda, Ganon, the central conflict, the stakes – everything we just defined. You'll actually tell the agent what roughly happens in each act, and then you'll ask it to draft that act, one at a time. So, it's a collaborative process where you guide the high-level plot. The `premise.json` literally gives it all the context it needs to make sure the story arc stays true to your initial vision. And once you've hashed out those acts, this agent then creates our next structured file for us: `arc.json`.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Arc.json' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, a code snippet is displayed against a dark background, formatted like a JSON file in a code editor. This is a continuation of the JSON content for the 'Arc' file. The visible JSON content shows an object representing 'act': 3. Inside this object, there are key-value pairs including 'label': 'Battle Aghanim and the Dark World', 'approx_pages_note': 'This act, encompassing the climax and a major cliffhanger, will likely be around 20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay.', 'dramatic_question': 'Can Link defeat Aghanim, save Zelda and the other maidens, and prevent Ganon from conquering their world, even when cast into the heart of darkness?', and a detailed 'summary' of the act. It also includes 'turning_point_end': 'Aghanim banishes Link to the corrupted Dark World, setting the stage for a new, desperate quest as the ultimate evil, Ganon, looms large.', and a list of 'must_hit_beats'. The JSON snippet ends with 'midpoint_note': null, and is not truncated at the bottom.
slides.narration:
Okay, so here it is, yeah? This is a snippet from our `arc.json` file. This is the output from our Arc Agent after you've discussed the overall story structure. And specifically, what we're looking at here is `act`: 3. This is the grand finale, the big conclusion for our movie!

You can see we've got a `label` here: 'Battle Aghanim and the Dark World.' And check out the `approx_pages_note`: 'This act... will likely be around 20-30 pages of a 120-page screenplay.' So, the agent is already giving us an idea of the scale and scope.

But the real kicker, the dramatic core of this act, is captured in the `dramatic_question`: 'Can Link defeat Aghanim, save Zelda and the other maidens, and prevent Ganon from conquering their world, even when cast into the heart of darkness?' That question is what drives the entire act. And below that, you've got a really detailed `summary` outlining Link's journey through this final act, from finding the Master Sword to confronting Aghanim.

Then, crucially, we have the `turning_point_end`. This is where it all changes, yeah? 'Aghanim banishes Link to the corrupted Dark World, setting the stage for a new, desperate quest as the ultimate evil, Ganon, looms large.' Talk about a cliffhanger! This isn't just an ending; it's a launchpad for more story.

And finally, you see the `must_hit_beats`. These are all the critical plot points, the things that absolutely *have* to happen within this act to fulfill the arc. So, this `arc.json` is giving us a incredibly detailed, coherent outline of our movie's story structure, all building directly on the `premise.json` we created earlier. This is the roadmap for our next agents.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Sequence Agent' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. In the top right corner, there is a small image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O cardboard robot from South Park. Below the title, there are four black, left-aligned bullet points. The first bullet states: 'An automated agent (no chat) that writes the sequences of the movie for each act'. The second bullet reads: 'Inputs per sequence'. This bullet has two indented sub-bullets: '– Premise.json, arc.json – give agent global view of the movie' and '– All previous sequences generated so far – give agent local context for what should happen next'. The third bullet states: 'Primary sequence elements'. This bullet has three indented sub-bullets: '– Goal – what is the narrative purpose of this sequence', '– Key locations – locations in the sequence', and '– Exit beat – how the sequence ends and transitions to next sequence'. The fourth and final bullet reads: 'This agent then creates a file sequence.json'.
slides.narration:
Okay, so we've got our `premise.json`, we've got our `arc.json` outlining the acts, right? Now we move into the next level of granularity, and this is where our 'Sequence Agent' comes in. And you see A.W.E.S.O.M.-O is still here, cheering us on. The big difference here, yeah? This is an *automated agent*. You're not chatting with this one. It's designed to take all that context and just start cranking out the sequences for each act of your movie.

So, what does this agent need as 'Inputs per sequence'? Well, first off, it gets that `premise.json` and the `arc.json`. That gives the agent the *global view* of the entire movie – all the core elements, the characters, the conflict, and the high-level plot for each act. But then, crucially, it also gets 'All previous sequences generated so far.' This is super important because it provides the agent with the *local context* for what should happen next. It helps maintain that coherence, ensuring the story flows logically from one sequence to the next.

And what does this agent actually *create* for each sequence? It defines the 'Primary sequence elements': things like the 'Goal' – what's the narrative purpose? What needs to be achieved in this chunk of the story? Then, the 'Key locations' where this sequence takes place. And finally, the 'Exit beat' – how does this sequence end and transition smoothly into the next one?

So, this agent takes all that rich context, both global and local, and breaks down those acts into these manageable, structured sequences. And once it's done its magic, it creates another structured file for us: `sequence.json`.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Sequence.json' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, a code snippet is displayed against a dark background, formatted like a JSON file in a code editor. The visible JSON content begins with 'project_id': 'shadow_of_the_dark_world_20260402_025456', 'sequence_count': 8, and 'note': null. It then shows the start of a 'sequences' array, containing an object for the first sequence. This object has 'id': 'SEQ1', 'act': 1, 'title': 'Zelda's Urgent Plea', and a 'goal' described as 'To establish Link’s call to adventure, his initial motivation (saving his uncle and Zelda), and to equip him with his first tools for the perilous journey ahead.' It also includes 'emotional_tone': 'Urgent, sorrowful, determined', 'key_locations' as an array listing 'Link’s Home' and 'Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls)', and an 'exit_beat' stating: 'Armed with his uncle’s sword and shield, Link steels himself, ready to infiltrate the formidable Hyrule Castle and rescue Princess Zelda.'
slides.narration: Okay, so here it is, yeah? This is what our automated Sequence Agent actually *gives* us. This is a snippet from our `sequence.json` file, and you can see how it's breaking down one of the acts into these digestible story units. Up top, we've got our `project_id` and the `sequence_count` – eight sequences in this movie, cool. Now, let's look at this first one, `SEQ1`, which is part of `act`: 1. The `title` is 'Zelda's Urgent Plea.' But look at the `goal`: 'To establish Link’s call to adventure, his initial motivation (saving his uncle and Zelda), and to equip him with his first tools for the perilous journey ahead.' That's a clear narrative purpose, right? It tells us exactly what this sequence needs to achieve. And we've even got an `emotional_tone` defined: 'Urgent, sorrowful, determined.' Plus, the `key_locations` are laid out: 'Link’s Home' and 'Hyrule Castle (Outer Walls).' And then, crucially, the `exit_beat`: 'Armed with his uncle’s sword and shield, Link steels himself, ready to infiltrate the formidable Hyrule Castle and rescue Princess Zelda.' See how detailed that is? This `sequence.json` is giving us all the necessary building blocks to move from the high-level act outline down to individual scenes, ensuring everything stays coherent and purposeful.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Scene Agent' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. In the top right corner, there is a small image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O cardboard robot from South Park. Below the title, there are four black, left-aligned bullet points. The first bullet states: 'An automated agent (no chat) that writes the scenes of the movie for each sequence'. The second bullet reads: 'Inputs per sequence'. This bullet has two indented sub-bullets: '– Premise.json, arc.json, sequence.json – give agent global view of the movie' and '– All scenes generated so far in this sequence and the previous sequence – give agent local context for what should happen next'. The third bullet states: 'Primary scene elements'. This bullet has two indented sub-bullets: '– Slug line – location of the scene' and '– Blocks – series of actions and dialogues in the scene'. The fourth and final bullet reads: 'This agent then creates a file scenes.json'.
slides.narration:
Okay, so we've taken our movie from a high-level premise, to a story arc, down to detailed sequences, right? Now we're at the very bottom of that hierarchy, the most granular level, and this is where our 'Scene Agent' comes in. And yeah, A.W.E.S.O.M.-O is still with us, keeping an eye on things. This, just like the Sequence Agent, is an *automated agent*, cool? No more chatting here. Its job is to take all that amazing context we've built up and just *write* the actual scenes of the movie for each sequence.

So, what does this agent need as its 'Inputs per sequence' to do its magic? First off, it gets all the big picture stuff: your `premise.json`, your `arc.json`, and your `sequence.json`. That gives the agent the absolute *global view* of the entire movie – it knows the core idea, the act structure, and the goals of the current sequence. But then, critically, it also gets 'All scenes generated so far in this sequence and the previous sequence.' This is what provides that crucial *local context*, ensuring that the scenes flow logically, that characters are in the right place, and that the narrative makes sense from moment to moment.

And what does this agent actually generate for us? It defines the 'Primary scene elements.' These are things like the 'Slug line' – which tells you the location of the scene, like `INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT`. And then the 'Blocks' – which are the series of actions and dialogues that make up the actual scene. So, you're getting the full, detailed script.

And finally, once it's done processing all that context and generating those detailed moments, this agent then creates our most granular structured file: `scenes.json`.

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Scenes.json' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, a code snippet is displayed against a dark background, formatted like a JSON file in a code editor. The visible JSON content shows the beginning of a 'scenes' array. Inside this array, an object represents the first scene, with 'id': 'S0001', 'slug_id': 'SEQ1-01', 'slugline': 'INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT', and 'sequence_id': 'SEQ1'. It then shows a 'blocks' array, detailing the scene's action and dialogue. The first three blocks are 'action' describing Link sleeping, hearing a whisper, and waking up. The fourth block is 'dialogue' from 'ZELDA (V.O.)' saying 'Link... help me... I'm trapped in the castle... Aghanim... has me...'. The fifth block is 'action', describing Link clutching his head with visions of Hyrule Castle, Zelda, and a sorcerer. The JSON snippet is truncated at the bottom, indicating more content beyond the visible area.
slides.narration:
Okay, so here it is, yeah? This is the grand finale, the output from our Scene Agent: the `scenes.json` file. This is the actual screenplay text, broken down into individual scenes, with action and dialogue. This is the most granular level, the culmination of all the previous agents' work.

So, let's dive in. You can see we've got an `id` for the scene, `S0001`, and a `slug_id` linking it to our first sequence, `SEQ1-01`. Then, the crucial `slugline`: `INT. LINK'S HOME - NIGHT`. That's standard screenplay format, telling us where and when the scene takes place. And it confirms it's part of `sequence_id`: `SEQ1`.

Now, the real magic is in the `blocks` array, yeah? This is where the scene actually unfolds. The first block is `action`: 'A soft glow from a nearby lantern casts long shadows across the simple, familiar room. LINK, a young man with a kind face, sleeps soundly in his bed.' See how detailed that is? It's setting the scene perfectly.

Then, more `action`: 'Suddenly, a piercing, desperate WHISPER echoes in his mind, not heard through his ears, but felt deep in his skull.' And another: 'He jolts awake, eyes wide, disoriented. The whisper intensifies, forming words.' Building the tension, right?

And then, boom, we hit `dialogue`. Our `character`: 'ZELDA (V.O.)', meaning voice-over. And her `lines`: 'Link... help me... I'm trapped in the castle... Aghanim... has me...' Oh, the drama!

Finally, more `action`: 'Link clutches his head, a jolt of pain, as visions flash through his mind: the cold stone walls of HYRULE CASTLE, Princess Zelda’s terrified face, the menacing silhouette of a SORCERER.'

This `scenes.json` shows how all that context we built – the premise, the arc, the sequences – gets distilled into coherent, actionable screenplay format. This is the fully realized vision, thanks to our agentic flow, cool?

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Enhancement Agents' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, on the left, a black, left-aligned bullet point states: 'We can improve the agentic flow by using agents to enhance intermediate outputs'. This bullet has three indented sub-bullets: '– Write better sequences or scenes', '– Clean up the premise', and '– Make the arc more creative'. To the right of the bullet points, a small image of a golden robot labeled 'ENHANCEMENT AGENT' is displayed. Below these elements, a horizontal flowchart diagram, similar to a previous slide, illustrates the 'Screenplay Flow'. It shows a blue rectangular box labeled 'Premise' with 'premise.json', a purple rectangular box labeled 'Arc' with 'arc.json', an orange rectangular box labeled 'Sequences' with 'sequence.json', and a green rectangular box labeled 'Scenes' with 'scenes.json'. Each box has a small robot icon. Additionally, small robot icons are positioned on the arrows pointing between the 'Premise' and 'Arc' boxes, and between the 'Arc' and 'Scenes' boxes, suggesting 'enhancement agents' at these stages of the flow.
slides.narration: Okay, so we've just laid out the core agentic flow, right? Premise, Arc, Sequences, Scenes. But here's the cool part, the kicker: we can actually *improve* this whole agentic flow even further by dropping in what we call 'Enhancement Agents.' You see our little golden robot on the right, labeled 'ENHANCEMENT AGENT'? Yeah, that's what we're talking about. These are specialized agents whose job isn't to *create* new content from scratch, but to *refine* and *improve* the intermediate outputs from our other agents.

slides.description:
The slide is titled 'Generalizing to Other Documents' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, a black, left-aligned bullet point states: 'By modifying the agent prompts, this hierarchical flow can be used for other long text documents'. Below the bullet point, a table is displayed with six columns and six rows. The column headers are: 'Document', 'Premise', 'Arc', 'Sequence', 'Scene', and 'Artist Rendering'.

The first column, 'Document', lists five types of documents: 'Book', 'Podcast', 'Song', 'Lecture', and 'Business plan'.

The 'Book' row maps these stages to: 'Premise', 'Table of contents', 'Chapter', and 'Sub-chapter'. The 'Artist Rendering' column for 'Book' shows a small image of the A.W.E.S.O.M.-O cardboard robot.

The 'Podcast' row maps to: 'Concept', 'Flow, format', 'Segment', and 'Beat'. The 'Artist Rendering' column for 'Podcast' shows a small image of a robot sitting at a desk with headphones.

The 'Song' row maps to: 'Premise', 'Structure (verse, hooks, coda)', 'Verse', and 'Couplet'. The 'Artist Rendering' column for 'Song' shows a small image of a robot on a stage with musical instruments.

The 'Lecture' row maps to: 'Learning objective', 'Outline', 'Module', and 'Slide'. The 'Artist Rendering' column for 'Lecture' shows a small image of a robot giving a presentation at a podium.

The 'Business plan' row maps to: 'Value prop', 'Outline', 'Section', and 'Sub-section'. The 'Artist Rendering' column for 'Business plan' shows a small image of a robot in an office looking at a screen with a business plan.
slides.narration: Okay, so we just introduced the idea of Enhancement Agents to make our screenplay flow even better, right? But here's the *really* powerful part, and it's what this slide, 'Generalizing to Other Documents,' is all about. The core concept, that bullet point there: 'By modifying the agent prompts, this hierarchical flow can be used for other long text documents.' This isn't just about movies, yeah? This architecture is totally flexible and generalizable. We can adapt it for anything that has a hierarchical structure. You just tweak what each agent is prompting for. Look at the table here, cool? For a 'Book,' our 'Premise' becomes the 'Premise' of the book. Our 'Arc' becomes the 'Table of contents.' Our 'Sequence' agent generates a 'Chapter,' and our 'Scene' agent generates a 'Sub-chapter.' See how that maps? And for a 'Podcast,' we're looking at the 'Concept' as the premise, the 'Flow, format' as the arc, 'Segment' as the sequence, and a 'Beat' as the scene. We can even do this for 'Songs,' breaking it down into 'Premise,' 'Structure' (like verse, hooks, coda), 'Verse,' and then 'Couplet.' And hey, guess what? For a 'Lecture' like this one, the 'Learning objective' is the premise, the 'Outline' is the arc, a 'Module' is a sequence, and a 'Slide' is the scene. Even for a 'Business plan,' you've got your 'Value prop,' your 'Outline,' then 'Section,' and 'Sub-section.' So, this isn't just a trick for screenplays; it's a fundamental way to tackle *any* complex, long-form document generation using AI, just by adjusting the prompts each agent uses. Pretty wild, right?

slides.description: The slide is titled 'Coding Session' in large, bold blue text, centered at the top. Below the title, there are three black, left-aligned bullet points. The first bullet states: 'Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O'. The second bullet reads: 'Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow'. The third and final bullet states: 'Modify Awesome-O for different document types'. Below the bullet points, a horizontal flowchart diagram, similar to previous 'Screenplay Flow' slides, illustrates the document generation process. It shows a blue rectangular box labeled 'Premise' with 'premise.json', a purple rectangular box labeled 'Arc' with 'arc.json', an orange rectangular box labeled 'Sequences' with 'sequence.json', and a green rectangular box labeled 'Scenes' with 'scenes.json'. Each box has a small robot icon. Additionally, small robot icons are positioned on the arrows pointing between the 'Premise' and 'Arc' boxes, between the 'Arc' and 'Sequences' boxes, and between the 'Arc' and 'Scenes' boxes, representing the enhancement agents at these stages.
slides.narration: Alright, so we've covered a *ton* of ground, yeah? From the high-level concept all the way down to individual scenes. And now, this is where the real fun begins: our 'Coding Session'! This is your chance to get hands-on and actually *build* what we've been talking about. So, what are we going to do? First off, we're going to 'Write a short screenplay using Awesome-O'. You'll get to interact with those chat agents and see the automated ones generate that `scenes.json` for you. It's super cool to see your ideas come to life. Second, we're going to 'Add enhancement agents to the agentic flow'. Remember those little golden robots on the arrows in our diagram here? You'll get to experiment with how they can refine and improve the intermediate outputs, making your screenplay even better. And finally, and this is truly exciting, you'll 'Modify Awesome-O for different document types'. We just talked about generalizing this whole architecture to books, podcasts, business plans – now you'll actually get to tweak the prompts and see how versatile this framework really is. So, let's get coding and bring these AI screenplays to life, yeah?
